items.passage: Many animals travel enormous distances each year to find food, warmer weather, or safe breeding grounds. Birds often follow the same routes that their ancestors used for countless generations before them. Some species navigate using sunlight, while others can sense the magnetic field of the planet. These remarkable journeys are triggered by changes in daylight and falling temperatures each autumn. Scientists believe that migration greatly improves the chances of survival for many different creatures.

items.passage: Sustainable agriculture aims to grow enough food today without damaging the land that future farmers will need. Farmers often rotate their crops so that the soil keeps its natural richness over many years. Cover plants are grown between harvests to protect the ground from wind and heavy rain. Although modern methods can raise yields quickly, they sometimes strip the soil of vital nutrients. Careful planning helps balance the demand for food with the health of the wider environment.

items.passage: The human body is wired to react to sudden danger with a rapid burst of physical changes. When a threat appears, sharp signals race through the brain and release hormones into the blood. A pounding heart then drives fresh energy toward the muscles, preparing the body for quick action. Although this reaction once shielded early humans from predators, it can be harmful when triggered too often. Long periods of stress may weaken the immune system and disturb normal patterns of sleep.

items.passage: The electric telegraph was built to carry messages across great distances almost instantly. Before this invention, news often moved no faster than a rider on horseback could travel. Operators tapped out coded signals that raced along metal wires between distant towns and busy cities. Although the system seems simple today, it once linked entire continents for the very first time. The telegraph laid the groundwork for the telephones and networks that societies depend on now.

items.passage: A river delta is formed where a slow river meets the sea and drops the sediment it carries. Over many centuries these deposits build broad plains of rich soil near its wide, shifting mouth. The water splits into smaller channels that spread across the land like the branches of a tree. Because the ground is flat and fertile, deltas often support dense villages and productive farms. However, rising seas and shrinking sediment now threaten many of these low regions around the world.

items.passage: Stars are formed inside vast clouds of gas and dust that drift quietly through empty space. Over time gravity pulls the scattered matter inward until a dense core builds near the middle. This core often grows hotter and denser, and in the end it ignites into a young sun. Although the process usually unfolds over millions of years, it repeats across every region of the galaxy. The largest stars burn through their fuel quickly, while smaller ones shine for billions of years.
